APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem (neural semantic parsing to SQL) and proposes an interactive, feedback-driven learning loop that is directly relevant to top conference audiences in semantic parsing and NLP systems. It uses appropriate benchmarks (GEO880, ATIS), execution-based accuracy, and a live deployment to demonstrate domain adaptation and annotation-effort tradeoffs, matching empirical standards expected at this venue. The treatment of noisy user feedback, bootstrapping with schema-derived templates and paraphrasing, and comparisons to prior semantic-parsing baselines position the work squarely within the community’s interests and evaluation norms. | B(5): This work sits squarely in the core NLP/ML semantic parsing and NLIDB research agenda: it proposes a neural seq2seq-to-SQL model plus an interactive feedback-and-crowd-annotation loop and validates on standard benchmarks (GEO880, ATIS) and a live deployment. The methodology (execution-based accuracy, user judgments, annotation-effort analysis) and comparisons to established baselines match community expectations. The paper targets the right audience by addressing practical domain adaptation, bootstrapping from schema templates and paraphrasing, and discussing real-world limitations (noisy feedback, non-executable queries). Overall it is highly relevant and well positioned for a top-tier NLP/ML venue.

The methodology (execution-based accuracy, user judgments, annotation-effort analysis) and comparisons to established baselines match community expectations. The paper targets the right audience by addressing practical domain adaptation, bootstrapping from schema templates and paraphrasing, and discussing real-world limitations (noisy feedback, non-executable queries). ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper brings a notable and practically useful combination of ideas: direct neural seq2seq mapping to SQL (avoiding intermediate representations) together with an interactive, feedback-driven annotation loop that leverages cheap binary user feedback plus selective crowd annotation for failures. While the model components (seq2seq, attention, entity anonymization, paraphrasing) are largely established, their integration into a live deployment pipeline that demonstrably bootstraps from synthetic schema templates and reduces annotation effort is a clear, non-trivial contribution that would inform practitioners and researchers. The work is thus more than incremental engineering but not a radical methodological departure from existing techniques. | B(3): The paper combines several known components (neural seq2seq semantic parsing, entity anonymization, template-based bootstrapping and paraphrasing) into a practical pipeline, and its core technical novelty is modest. Its more original aspects are the pragmatic deployment loop—learning from binary end-user feedback plus selective crowd annotation—and demonstrating direct SQL generation in a live NLIDB, which is useful but not a fundamentally new modeling paradigm compared to prior neural semantic parsing work (e.g., Dong & Lapata). Overall this reads as a solid, application-driven integration with moderate novelty rather than a highly novel methodological advance.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper brings a notable and practically useful combination of ideas: direct neural seq2seq mapping to SQL (avoiding intermediate representations) together with an interactive, feedback-driven annotation loop that leverages cheap binary user feedback plus selective crowd annotation for failures. The work is thus more than incremental engineering but not a radical methodological departure from existing techniques.

Its more original aspects are the pragmatic deployment loop—learning from binary end-user feedback plus selective crowd annotation—and demonstrating direct SQL generation in a live NLIDB, which is useful but not a fundamentally new modeling paradigm compared to prior neural semantic parsing work (e.g., Dong & Lapata). Overall this reads as a solid, application-driven integration with moderate novelty rather than a highly novel methodological advance.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (deployment loop, bootstrapping with templates, and feedback-driven annotation), which gives a logical intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow. Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear consistent and sufficient for reproducibility, and the experiments/tables (execution-based accuracy, user-judged correctness, annotation effort) are informative for the claims. Writing quality is good and related work coverage is thorough, though a few sections (e.g., limitations of templates on ATIS and details about non-executing queries) could use slightly clearer exposition to aid readers reproducing the live deployment. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early, making the overall narrative easy to follow. Notation is reported as consistent and hyperparameters are included; figures, tables, and examples (including execution-based evaluation and user-judged correctness) are informative. Writing quality is good with thorough related-work coverage, though a few presentation choices could be clearer (e.g., explicit mention of code availability and more detail on handling noisy user feedback and non-executing queries). Overall the presentation is strong with only minor issues.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (deployment loop, bootstrapping with templates, and feedback-driven annotation), which gives a logical intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow. Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear consistent and sufficient for reproducibility, and the experiments/tables (execution-based accuracy, user-judged correctness, annotation effort) are informative for the claims. Overall the presentation is strong with only minor issues.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important practical problem of deploying NL-to-SQL systems and demonstrates a realistic, iterative pipeline (seq2seq SQL model + binary user feedback + crowd annotation) that enables rapid domain adaptation and improves accuracy in a live system (63.51% user-judged correctness after three stages). The combination of bootstrapping via schema-derived templates and paraphrasing plus execution-based evaluation and comparisons to GEO880/ATIS baselines makes the approach compelling for practitioners. Limitations (noisy user feedback, template utility varying by dataset, execution failures) are acknowledged, but the demonstrated deployment loop and reduced annotation effort are likely to influence how researchers and practitioners build adaptive NLIDB systems and inspire follow-up work on denoising feedback and more efficient annotation strategies. | B(4): The paper tackles an important, practical problem—deploying NL→SQL interfaces with minimal annotation—and demonstrates a concrete, reusable recipe (seq2seq to SQL + bootstrap templates + binary user feedback + crowd correction) that achieves competitive benchmark accuracy and a working live deployment (63.5% user-judged correctness after iterations). Its emphasis on an iterative, low-cost annotation loop and schema-driven augmentation is likely to change practitioner workflows and motivate follow-on work on in-production learning for NLIDBs. Limitations noted (noisy feedback, template utility varying by domain, execution failures) temper claims of universality, so the contribution is strong and practically influential but not fully transformative across all semantic parsing settings.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important practical problem of deploying NL-to-SQL systems and demonstrates a realistic, iterative pipeline (seq2seq SQL model + binary user feedback + crowd annotation) that enables rapid domain adaptation and improves accuracy in a live system (63.51% user-judged correctness after three stages). The combination of bootstrapping via schema-derived templates and paraphrasing plus execution-based evaluation and comparisons to GEO880/ATIS baselines makes the approach compelling for practitioners. Limitations (noisy user feedback, template utility varying by dataset, execution failures) are acknowledged, but the demonstrated deployment loop and reduced annotation effort are likely to influence how researchers and practitioners build adaptive NLIDB systems and inspire follow-up work on denoising feedback and more efficient annotation strategies.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important, practical problem—deploying NL→SQL interfaces with minimal annotation—and demonstrates a concrete, reusable recipe (seq2seq to SQL + bootstrap templates + binary user feedback + crowd correction) that achieves competitive benchmark accuracy and a working live deployment (63.5% user-judged correctness after iterations). Its emphasis on an iterative, low-cost annotation loop and schema-driven augmentation is likely to change practitioner workflows and motivate follow-on work on in-production learning for NLIDBs. Limitations noted (noisy feedback, template utility varying by domain, execution failures) temper claims of universality, so the contribution is strong and practically influential but not fully transformative across all semantic parsing settings.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experimental program is solid and largely supports the main claims: bootstrapping with schema-derived templates and paraphrasing, live deployment with binary user feedback plus crowd annotation, and execution-based accuracy comparisons on GEO880 and ATIS are all present. The paper reports important failure modes (noisy user feedback, non-executing queries, template utility diminishing with more labeled data) and gives hyperparameters and annotation-effort statistics, which aids reproducibility, but code and some annotation-protocol details are not mentioned. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is partial (e.g., templates helped in some settings but not ATIS, and benefit wanes with more data) rather than exhaustive, and I see no discussion of statistical significance or deeper error breakdowns by query type, so additional analyses would be needed for a fully comprehensive evaluation. | B(4): The paper presents a convincing combination of offline benchmark experiments (GEO880, ATIS) and a real-world live deployment, showing the interactive feedback loop and data-augmentation strategies materially improve SQL accuracy — this breadth supports the main claims. It includes useful sensitivity insights (templates/paraphrasing help bootstrap but diminish with more data; anonymization and paraphrase methods are described), and it quantifies practical issues such as noisy user feedback and non-executable predictions. Related work is treated thoroughly and limitations are explicitly discussed, but reproducibility is slightly weak (no public code noted despite hyperparameters being reported) and some ablations (e.g., detailed impact of different crowd annotation qualities or alternative feedback types) are missing, so the empirical treatment is strong but not exhaustively comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental program is solid and largely supports the main claims: bootstrapping with schema-derived templates and paraphrasing, live deployment with binary user feedback plus crowd annotation, and execution-based accuracy comparisons on GEO880 and ATIS are all present. The paper reports important failure modes (noisy user feedback, non-executing queries, template utility diminishing with more labeled data) and gives hyperparameters and annotation-effort statistics, which aids reproducibility, but code and some annotation-protocol details are not mentioned. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is partial (e.g., templates helped in some settings but not ATIS, and benefit wanes with more data) rather than exhaustive, and I see no discussion of statistical significance or deeper error breakdowns by query type, so additional analyses would be needed for a fully comprehensive evaluation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a convincing combination of offline benchmark experiments (GEO880, ATIS) and a real-world live deployment, showing the interactive feedback loop and data-augmentation strategies materially improve SQL accuracy — this breadth supports the main claims. It includes useful sensitivity insights (templates/paraphrasing help bootstrap but diminish with more data; anonymization and paraphrase methods are described), and it quantifies practical issues such as noisy user feedback and non-executable predictions. Related work is treated thoroughly and limitations are explicitly discussed, but reproducibility is slightly weak (no public code noted despite hyperparameters being reported) and some ablations (e.g., detailed impact of different crowd annotation qualities or alternative feedback types) are missing, so the empirical treatment is strong but not exhaustively comprehensive.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against several well-known semantic parsing baselines (Dong & Lapata 2016; Jia & Liang 2016; GUSP; Zettlemoyer & Collins) and evaluates on standard datasets (GEO880, ATIS) using execution-based accuracy, which supports largely fair comparisons. It also acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably and analyzes how its interactive, feedback-driven training differs from batch baselines. However, the experimental account lacks details on statistical significance and does not appear to include some more recent neural baselines or open-source implementations for direct replication, so comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive. | B(5): The paper compares against a strong set of relevant baselines including recent neural approaches (Dong & Lapata 2016; Jia & Liang 2016) as well as classic NLIDB methods (Poon 2013; Zettlemoyer & Collins). Evaluations use standard datasets (GEO880, ATIS) and execution-based accuracy metrics, and the paper acknowledges cases where prior methods perform comparably while clearly analyzing how its interactive, feedback-driven workflow and data-augmentation differ. Related work coverage is described as thorough and limitations (noise in user feedback, template utility) are explicitly discussed, supporting a comprehensive and fair comparison.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against several well-known semantic parsing baselines (Dong & Lapata 2016; Jia & Liang 2016; GUSP; Zettlemoyer & Collins) and evaluates on standard datasets (GEO880, ATIS) using execution-based accuracy, which supports largely fair comparisons. It also acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably and analyzes how its interactive, feedback-driven training differs from batch baselines. However, the experimental account lacks details on statistical significance and does not appear to include some more recent neural baselines or open-source implementations for direct replication, so comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a strong set of relevant baselines including recent neural approaches (Dong & Lapata 2016; Jia & Liang 2016) as well as classic NLIDB methods (Poon 2013; Zettlemoyer & Collins). Evaluations use standard datasets (GEO880, ATIS) and execution-based accuracy metrics, and the paper acknowledges cases where prior methods perform comparably while clearly analyzing how its interactive, feedback-driven workflow and data-augmentation differ. Related work coverage is described as thorough and limitations (noise in user feedback, template utility) are explicitly discussed, supporting a comprehensive and fair comparison.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are largely supported: a seq2seq model trained directly to SQL is evaluated against standard baselines (GEO880, ATIS) with execution-based accuracy and further validated in a live deployment (63.51% user-judged correctness after iterative feedback). The evaluation methodology is generally sound—reasonable baselines and an interactive learning loop are included—but the summary lacks details on statistical significance, annotation costs/quality (IAA) and reproducibility (code not mentioned), and the authors themselves report notable issues (noisy user feedback and a substantial fraction of non-executing queries). These are minor but important gaps that do not invalidate the core technical contribution but limit confidence in some empirical claims. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by experiments on GEO880, ATIS and a live deployment, with execution-based accuracy and user-judged correctness reported; bootstrapping and interactive feedback are evaluated and ablation-style findings (templates, paraphrasing, anonymization) are presented. There are no formal mathematical proofs to check, and the neural seq2seq methodology is standard and correctly applied, but the experiment reporting omits statistical significance testing and relies on noisy binary user feedback (noted by the authors) which introduces potential bias in the iterative loop. Baselines are appropriate and execution-based evaluation is correct, yet the dependence on crowd annotation for negatives and non-executing queries (22.3%) reveal practical limitations that are not fully resolved. Overall the technical claims are supported, with minor methodological concerns about feedback noise and missing statistical analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are largely supported: a seq2seq model trained directly to SQL is evaluated against standard baselines (GEO880, ATIS) with execution-based accuracy and further validated in a live deployment (63.51% user-judged correctness after iterative feedback). The evaluation methodology is generally sound—reasonable baselines and an interactive learning loop are included—but the summary lacks details on statistical significance, annotation costs/quality (IAA) and reproducibility (code not mentioned), and the authors themselves report notable issues (noisy user feedback and a substantial fraction of non-executing queries). These are minor but important gaps that do not invalidate the core technical contribution but limit confidence in some empirical claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by experiments on GEO880, ATIS and a live deployment, with execution-based accuracy and user-judged correctness reported; bootstrapping and interactive feedback are evaluated and ablation-style findings (templates, paraphrasing, anonymization) are presented. There are no formal mathematical proofs to check, and the neural seq2seq methodology is standard and correctly applied, but the experiment reporting omits statistical significance testing and relies on noisy binary user feedback (noted by the authors) which introduces potential bias in the iterative loop. Baselines are appropriate and execution-based evaluation is correct, yet the dependence on crowd annotation for negatives and non-executing queries (22.3%) reveal practical limitations that are not fully resolved. Overall the technical claims are supported, with minor methodological concerns about feedback noise and missing statistical analysis.

main_claims: A neural sequence-to-sequence model can be trained to map natural language utterances directly to SQL, enabling deployment of NL interfaces to databases without intermediate meaning representations.

An interactive feedback-based learning loop that collects binary user feedback and uses crowd annotation for incorrect predictions allows rapid domain adaptation and improves parser accuracy over successive deployments.

Data augmentation via schema-derived templates, paraphrasing (PPDB), and entity anonymization help bootstrap the system and reduce annotation effort, enabling reasonable performance even with initially synthetic training data.

The approach achieves comparable accuracy to prior work on benchmark semantic parsing datasets (GEO880 and ATIS) and can be learned from scratch in a live system (reaching 63.51% user-judged correctness after three stages).
_summary.methodology: Train an encoder-decoder neural seq2seq model with global attention to directly predict SQL from anonymized utterances, bootstrap training with synthetic schema-template examples and paraphrases, then iteratively deploy the model, collect binary correct/incorrect user feedback, add correct model predictions to training data and obtain crowd-annotated SQL for incorrect predictions to retrain.
datasets: an online academic database (live interactive experiment domain)
metrics: Accuracy measured by executing predicted SQL and comparing results to gold (execution-based accuracy)
User-judged correctness (percentage of utterances marked Correct or Incomplete Result)
Percentage of examples requiring annotation (annotation effort)
limitations: User feedback is noisy: 6.1% of correct queries were marked wrong and 6.3% of incorrect queries were marked correct, causing redundant or harmful annotations.
Templates provided limited benefit on some datasets (e.g., did not improve ATIS) and their advantage decreases as more labeled data is collected.
Some generated queries did not execute (22.3% in final stage), and incomplete database content for aggregation queries caused mislabeling by users.
_summary.topic_area: Semantic parsing / Natural language interfaces to databases (NLIDB), mapping NL to SQL

reviewer_a.name: Empirical Methods Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Research scientist with a PhD in machine learning and 8+ years building and evaluating neural NLP systems; experience deploying production sequence-to-sequence models and running large-scale annotation pipelines.
traits: carefully examines experimental design, including train/validation/test splits, statistical significance, and potential leakage
expects clear baselines, ablation studies, and hyperparameter/optimization details to assess comparability and reproducibility
looks for rigorous evaluation metrics and error analyses that support the main claims (e.g., per-query-type performance, confidence calibration)
values transparency about annotation protocols, inter-annotator agreement, and costs of crowd-labeling and human-in-the-loop steps
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Practical, detail-oriented reviewer who focuses on reproducibility, robustness of empirical claims, and whether experiments convincingly support the conclusions.

reviewer_b.name: Semantic Parsing & NLIDB Specialist
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in semantic parsing and natural language interfaces to databases with a track record of publications on program synthesis, weak supervision, and user-in-the-loop learning for NLIDB systems.
traits: evaluates novelty relative to prior semantic parsing and interactive learning work, emphasizing conceptual advances and limitations
assesses practical impact for real-world NLIDB deployment, including scalability across schemas, language variability, and user experience
notices assumptions about annotation and user feedback (e.g., binary correctness), and how they affect generality and adoption
values clear positioning against related paradigms (indirect meaning representations, grammar-driven approaches, weak supervision) and discussion of failure modes
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: High-level, context-aware reviewer who judges the paper by its novelty, applicability, and how persuasively it addresses open problems in semantic parsing and NL interfaces.
